name: JavaScript
blurb: The language of the web — types, scope, closures, and the event loop.

cards.question: What is the JavaScript
cards.answer:
A high-level scripting language originally built to make web pages interactive.

High-level — abstracts away hardware. You write human-readable logic; the engine handles memory, CPU, and binary translation.

LevelExamples
HighJavaScript, Python, Java, C#
LowAssembly, C, C++

Script — plain-text instructions executed directly by the engine. No compilation, no build step.

// script.js — runs top to bottom as-is
console.log("Hello");   // engine reads and executes immediately

cards.question: Where did the name "JavaScript" come from?
cards.answer:
Originally called LiveScript — renamed to JavaScript purely for marketing, to ride Java's popularity at the time.

Despite the name, JavaScript has no relation to Java. Completely independent languages with different designs and purposes.

cards.question: What is ECMAScript
cards.answer:
ECMAScript is the official specification — the rulebook — that JavaScript follows. JavaScript came first (1995); ECMAScript was created in 1996 to standardize it.

The name exists because "JavaScript" is Oracle's trademark — ECMA International used "ECMAScript" to avoid legal issues.

TermDefinition
ECMAScriptThe specification that defines how the language should work
JavaScriptThe actual language developers use, which implements that specification

Maintained by TC39. Annual releases since 2015. ES5 added strict mode and JSON. ES6/ES2015 — also called ES6 — brought classes, arrow functions, modules. ES2025 is the current specification.

cards.question: How does a JavaScript engine work?
cards.answer:
A JavaScript engine reads your code in three steps:

  1. Parse — reads the script and checks the syntax
  2. Compile — converts it to machine code
  3. Execute — runs the machine code, fast

The engine doesn't stop there — it watches the code as it runs, analyzes the data flowing through it, and keeps optimizing the machine code on the fly.

Well-known engines: V8 (Chrome, Node.js), SpiderMonkey (Firefox).

This is a simplified view — engines are far more complex under the hood.

cards.question: What is JIT compilation?
cards.answer:
JIT (Just-In-Time) compilation is a technique that translates computer code directly into native machine code while the program is executing (at runtime).


Traditional compilers compile everything upfront. Interpreters run line by line without compiling. JIT is the middle ground — it compiles at runtime, right when the code is needed.


ApproachHow it works
CompilerCompiles everything upfront, then runs
InterpreterRuns line by line, no compilation
JITCompiles at runtime, right when needed

This is why JavaScript is fast despite being a scripting language. The engine also watches which parts run most often and optimizes those hotspots further.

cards.question: Is JavaScript interpreted or compiled?
cards.answer:
Neither purely. JavaScript is often called interpreted because there's no separate compile step — you write it and run it directly, unlike C or Java.

But modern engines use JIT — they compile the code under the hood at runtime. From the developer's side it feels interpreted; under the hood it's being compiled.

The correct answer: JavaScript is JIT-compiled at runtime.

cards.question: What can JavaScript do in the browser?
cards.answer:
JavaScript in the browser can:

  • Manipulate the page — add, change, or remove HTML and CSS
  • React to user actions — clicks, key presses, mouse movements
  • Communicate with servers — send and receive data without reloading the page (AJAX)
  • Manage data — read and write cookies, use localStorage
  • Access devices — camera, microphone (with user permission)

cards.question: What can't JavaScript do in the browser?
cards.answer:
JavaScript runs in a sandbox — it can't touch the OS or other sites freely.

  • File system — can't read or write files on your computer. Only gets file access if the user explicitly selects one.
  • Other tabs — a page can't read data from a tab on a different domain. evil.com can't peek at your open gmail.com tab.
  • Cross-domain requests — a script can freely talk to its own server. Talking to a different domain requires that server to explicitly allow it (CORS headers).

These restrictions exist only in the browser. Node.js has none of them.

cards.question: What makes JavaScript unique?
cards.answer:
No other browser technology combines all three:

  • Native HTML/CSS integration — built into the browser alongside them, no setup needed.
  • Simple things stay simple — basic tasks don't need boilerplate.
  • Runs everywhere by default — supported in all major browsers, enabled out of the box.

cards.question: What are transpiled languages?
cards.answer:
Some languages compile down to JavaScript so they can run in the browser. You write in another language, a tool converts it to JS automatically.

LanguageWhat it adds
TypeScriptStrict types on top of JS (Microsoft)
CoffeeScriptCleaner, shorter syntax — popular with Ruby devs
FlowType checking only (Facebook)
DartStandalone language by Google, can target the browser
KotlinModern language, can compile to JS
BabelTranspiles modern JS to older JS for browser compatibility

Whatever language you use, the browser still runs JavaScript underneath — that's why understanding JS itself matters.

cards.question: What is a variable?
cards.answer:
A variable is a named storage for data — a place to keep a value so you can refer to it later by name.

let message = 'Hello';
message = 'World';     // the stored value can be replaced
console.log(message);  // World

You can picture it as a box with a name sticker on it: the box holds the value, and the name lets you find it again.

cards.question: What are the three ways to declare a variable in JavaScript?
cards.answer:
A variable is a named storage for a value. You create one with const, let, or the older var.

The keyword sets how the variable behaves: where it is visible, whether its value can change, and how it acts before its declaration runs.

Visible inCan change?Redeclare?
constthe blocknono
letthe blockyesno
varthe whole functionyesyes

Today let and const are used for everything; var is the older form, kept for backward compatibility.

cards.question: What's the difference between block scope and function scope?
cards.answer:
Scope is the region of code where a variable is visible. let and const are block-scoped — they exist only inside the { } block where they are declared. var is function-scoped — it ignores blocks and exists throughout the whole function.

function f() {
  if (true) {
    var a = 1;
    let b = 2;
  }
  console.log(a); // 1 — var is visible here
  console.log(b); // ReferenceError — b only existed in the block
}

Because a var is not limited to its block, it stays visible after the block ends — a common source of bugs.

cards.question: What does each keyword allow for reassignment, redeclaration, and initialization?
cards.answer:
let can be reassigned and may be declared without a value. const must be given a value when declared and cannot be reassigned afterward. Neither can be declared twice in the same scope; var can.

const a;     // SyntaxError — const needs a value
let b;       // ok — b is undefined

let x = 1;
x = 2;       // ok — reassigned
let x = 3;   // SyntaxError — already declared

var y = 1;
var y = 2;   // ok — var allows redeclaration

Redeclaring the same name is usually a mistake, so let and const report it as an error.

cards.question: Does const make the value immutable?
cards.answer:
const stops the variable from being reassigned — it does not make the value itself read-only. If a const holds an object or array, its contents can still be changed.

The variable is a sticker on a box: const stops you moving the sticker to another box, but the box can still be filled and emptied.

const arr = [1, 2];
arr.push(3);   // ok — changing the contents
arr = [];      // TypeError — reassigning the variable

To freeze the contents as well, use Object.freeze.

cards.question: What are the rules and conventions for naming variables?
cards.answer:
A name may contain only letters, digits, and the symbols $ and _, and the first character cannot be a digit. Names are case-sensitive, and reserved words like let or class cannot be used.

let userName;   // ok
let $el, _x;    // ok
let 1st;        // SyntaxError — starts with a digit
let name, Name; // two different variables

By convention, use camelCase for variables and UPPER_CASE for hard-coded constants.

cards.question: Which declaration should you reach for by default, and why?
cards.answer:
Use const by default, and let only for values that need to change. Avoid var.

Defaulting to const makes it clear which values are meant to stay fixed, and both let and const avoid the function-scope and hoisting quirks of var.

cards.question: What is the difference between a statement and an expression?
cards.answer:
An expression is any code that produces a value. A statement is an instruction that performs an action. A program is a list of statements.

2 + 2          // expression → produces 4
let x = 5;     // statement → performs a declaration

Expressions appear inside statements: in let x = 2 + 2; the right side is an expression, the whole line is a statement.

cards.question: What is an expression statement?
cards.answer:
When an expression stands on its own as a statement, it is an expression statement: its value is computed and then discarded, so it is only useful for its side effect.

greet();   // function call — kept for its side effect
x = 3;     // assignment expression used as a statement
x + 1;     // valid, but pointless — the value is thrown away

cards.question: How do you produce a value where a statement like if won't work?
cards.answer:
Statements such as if and for do not produce values, so they cannot be used where a value is expected. Use an expression form instead: the ternary ? :, or && / ||.

const max = a > b ? a : b;   // ok — ternary is an expression
const max = if (a > b) a;    // SyntaxError — if is a statement

cards.question: Why can the same characters mean different things depending on position?
cards.answer:
Position decides meaning. At the start of a statement { begins a block; in an expression position the same { begins an object literal.

{}              // an empty block
const o = {};   // an object literal

This is why an object literal returned from an arrow function must be wrapped: () => ({}).

cards.question: What is strict mode and how do you enable it?
cards.answer:
Strict mode is a restricted variant of JavaScript that makes the engine stricter about mistakes. You enable it with the directive 'use strict' at the top of a script or a function body.

'use strict';
// ... the rest of the file runs in strict mode

Modern code rarely writes it: ES modules and class bodies are in strict mode automatically.

cards.question: What does strict mode change?
cards.answer:
It turns silent mistakes into thrown errors and removes some unsafe behavior.

  • Assigning to an undeclared variable throws, instead of silently creating a global.
  • Writing to a read-only property throws instead of failing quietly.
  • Duplicate function parameter names become a syntax error.
  • this is undefined in a plain function call, not the global object.
  • The old with statement is forbidden.

cards.question: Why does strict mode exist, and should you use it?
cards.answer:
It surfaces bugs early by refusing to ignore questionable code, and it disables legacy behavior that made code harder to optimize and reason about.

In practice you get it for free — modules and classes are always strict — so you only add 'use strict' in old-style, non-module scripts.

cards.question: What are operands, and what are unary, binary, and ternary operators?
cards.answer:
An operator acts on values called operands. Operators are grouped by how many operands they take: unary (one), binary (two), and the single ternary (three).

-x          // unary minus (one operand)
a + b       // binary plus (two operands)
a ? b : c   // the ternary operator (three operands)

cards.question: What do the arithmetic operators do, and how does + behave with strings?
cards.answer:
The arithmetic operators are + - * / plus % (remainder) and ** (exponentiation). They all convert their operands to numbers — except +, which concatenates if either operand is a string.

2 ** 3     // 8
10 % 3     // 1
'1' + 2    // '12'  — string concatenation
'5' - 2    // 3     — converted to numbers

cards.question: What do the logical operators && || ! return, and what is short-circuiting?
cards.answer:
The logical operators are && (and), || (or), and ! (not). && and || do not return a boolean — they return one of the operands, and they short-circuit, stopping as soon as the result is known.

a && b   // a if a is falsy, otherwise b
a || b   // a if a is truthy, otherwise b
'' || 'default'   // 'default'

This lets you write user.name || 'Guest' or isReady && render().

cards.question: What are operator precedence and associativity?
cards.answer:
Precedence decides which operator runs first (* before +). Associativity decides the order when operators share precedence: most are left-to-right, but assignment is right-to-left. Parentheses override both.

2 + 3 * 4    // 14, not 20
a = b = 5    // right-to-left: b = 5, then a = 5

cards.question: What's the difference between x++ and ++x?
cards.answer:
++ and -- change a variable by one. Prefix (++x) changes it and returns the new value; postfix (x++) returns the old value and then changes it.

let a = 1;
let b = a++;   // b = 1, a = 2
let c = ++a;   // c = 3, a = 3

cards.question: What does the nullish coalescing operator ?? do?
cards.answer:
a ?? b returns b only when a is null or undefined. It is like ||, but it does not trigger on other falsy values such as 0 or ''.

0 || 5     // 5  — || treats 0 as missing
0 ?? 5     // 0  — ?? only fills in null / undefined

Use ?? for defaults when 0, '', or false are valid values.

cards.question: What does optional chaining ?. do?
cards.answer:
a?.b reads b only if a is not null or undefined; otherwise the whole expression short-circuits to undefined instead of throwing. It works for properties, calls, and dynamic keys.

user?.address?.city   // undefined if user or address is missing
user.getName?.()      // called only if getName exists
arr?.[0]              // index access, guarded

Use it for genuinely optional parts — not to paper over data that should always be there.

cards.question: What do the logical assignment operators ??= ||= &&= do?
cards.answer:
They combine a logical operator with assignment. x ??= y assigns y only if x is null/undefined; ||= assigns if x is falsy; &&= assigns if x is truthy.

let opts = {};
opts.size ??= 'M';   // set only if not already set

Each short-circuits, so the right side runs only when the assignment actually happens.

cards.question: What are the conditional statements?
cards.answer:
if runs a block when its condition is truthy; else and else if handle the alternatives. To choose between two values, the ternary cond ? a : b is the expression form.

if (score >= 50) pass();
else fail();

const grade = score >= 50 ? 'pass' : 'fail';

cards.question: How does switch work, and what is fall-through?
cards.answer:
switch compares one value against several case labels using strict equality (===). Execution falls through into the next case unless you break, and default handles no match.

switch (day) {
  case 6:
  case 0:
    return 'weekend';   // shared via fall-through
  default:
    return 'weekday';
}

Forgetting break is a classic bug — execution keeps running into the next case.

cards.question: What are the loop statements: while, do...while, and for?
cards.answer:
while repeats while its condition holds, checked before each pass. do...while checks after the body, so it always runs at least once. for packs setup, condition, and step into one header.

for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) { /* ... */ }
while (cond) { /* ... */ }
do { /* ... */ } while (cond);

cards.question: What's the difference between for...of and for...in?
cards.answer:
for...of loops over the values of an iterable (array, string, Map, Set). for...in loops over the keys of an object — including inherited ones — and is not meant for arrays.

for (const x of [10, 20]) { } // 10, 20  (values)
for (const k in obj) { }      // 'a', 'b' (keys)

cards.question: What do break and continue do, and what are labels?
cards.answer:
break stops a loop entirely; continue skips to the next iteration. A label lets break or continue target an outer loop from inside a nested one.

outer:
for (const row of grid) {
  for (const cell of row) {
    if (cell === target) break outer;
  }
}

cards.question: Are semicolons required in JavaScript?
cards.answer:
Mostly no. JavaScript has Automatic Semicolon Insertion (ASI): the parser inserts semicolons at most line breaks, so you can usually omit them.

let a = 1   // works — ASI adds the semicolon
let b = 2

cards.question: When does ASI cause bugs?
cards.answer:
ASI follows fixed rules, and a few cases break code. The worst is return with its value on the next line — ASI ends the statement right after return, so it returns undefined.

return
  value;   // returns undefined, not value

Lines starting with ( [ or a backtick can also merge with the previous line.

cards.question: What's the practical rule for semicolons?
cards.answer:
Pick one style and stay consistent. If you omit semicolons, guard the risky lines by starting any line that begins with ( or [ with a semicolon.

;[1, 2].forEach(fn)   // leading ; stops it merging with the line above

Most teams let a formatter (Prettier) settle the choice automatically.
